events.title: Battle of Gonzales — 'Come and Take It'
events.detail: Mexican soldiers attempted to reclaim a small bronze cannon loaned to settlers at Gonzales, Texas. Texan colonists refused, raised a defiant flag reading 'Come and Take It,' and repelled the Mexican detachment in a brief skirmish. Widely considered the opening engagement of the Texas Revolution, the cannon remained in Texan hands.

sources.name: Texas State Library and Archives Commission
Stephen L. Hardin, Texian Iliad (University of Texas Press)

events.title: Texans Capture La Bahía (Goliad)
events.detail: A Texan force under Captain George Collinsworth stormed the presidio La Bahía at Goliad, capturing the Mexican garrison and a substantial supply of arms and gunpowder. The victory gave Texans control of the only port on the lower coast and secured crucial military supplies for the revolution.

sources.name: Texas State Library and Archives Commission
Paul D. Lack, The Texas Revolutionary Experience (Texas A&M University Press)

events.title: Battle of Concepción
events.detail: Near the mission Concepción outside San Antonio, James Bowie and James Fannin's Texan force used the natural terrain to repel a larger Mexican force under General Cos. Texan riflemen inflicted heavy casualties while suffering minimal losses, demonstrating that irregular Texan tactics could defeat regular Mexican troops in the field.

sources.name: Stephen L. Hardin, Texian Iliad (University of Texas Press)
Daughters of the Republic of Texas Library

events.title: Consultation of 1835 — Texan Provisional Government Formed
events.detail: Texan delegates meeting at San Felipe de Austin established a provisional government, named Henry Smith as provisional governor, and Sam Houston as commander-in-chief of the Texas army. The Consultation initially declined to declare independence but issued a Declaration of Causes justifying armed resistance. It marked the institutional birth of the Texas Revolution's governing structure.

sources.name: Texas State Library and Archives Commission — Consultation Records
Eugene C. Barker, The Life of Stephen F. Austin (University of Texas Press)

events.title: The Grass Fight Near San Antonio
events.detail: Texan scouts under James Bowie attacked a Mexican pack-mule train near San Antonio believing it carried silver payroll. The mules instead carried grass fodder for horses, but the engagement revealed severe supply difficulties facing the Mexican garrison under General Martín Perfecto de Cos inside Bexar.

sources.name: Stephen L. Hardin, Texian Iliad (University of Texas Press)
Handbook of Texas Online — Texas State Historical Association

events.title: Storming of Bexar — Texans Capture San Antonio
events.detail: After weeks of siege, Texan volunteers launched a house-to-house assault on San Antonio de Bexar under Benjamin Milam (who was killed in the fighting) and Frank Johnson. General Cos surrendered the city and the Alamo fortress on December 10, signing a parole agreement to withdraw his forces south of the Rio Grande. Texans now controlled all of Texas north of that river.

sources.name: Texas General Land Office — Historical Records
Stephen L. Hardin, Texian Iliad (University of Texas Press)

era: Texas Revolution — Independence, Alamo & San Jacinto (1836)

events.title: Santa Anna Marches North to Crush the Rebellion
events.detail: President-General Antonio López de Santa Anna personally led an army of approximately 6,000 soldiers from San Luis Potosí northward to Texas. He declared the Texan rebels 'pirates' subject to execution under Mexican law, and vowed to expel or destroy all Anglo-American colonists. His winter march across the arid Coahuila plains caused significant attrition to his forces before battle was joined.

sources.name: Will Fowler, Santa Anna of Mexico (University of Nebraska Press)
Archivo General de la Nación, Mexico City

events.title: Texas Declaration of Independence
events.detail: Delegates at the Convention of 1836, meeting at Washington-on-the-Brazos, unanimously adopted a Declaration of Independence modeled on the US Declaration. George Childress was the primary author. The declaration accused the Mexican government of imposing military despotism and denying constitutional rights. Texas formally became the Republic of Texas — though the revolution was still undecided on the battlefield.

sources.name: Texas Declaration of Independence — Texas State Library
William C. Davis, Three Roads to the Alamo (HarperCollins)

events.title: Siege of the Alamo Begins
events.detail: Santa Anna's vanguard reached San Antonio on February 23, 1836, catching the garrison under Lt. Col. William Barret Travis and Jim Bowie largely unprepared. Approximately 182–189 defenders occupied the old mission converted to a fort. Travis sent his famous letter appealing for reinforcements: 'I shall never surrender or retreat... Victory or Death.' A small party of 32 volunteers from Gonzales slipped through to reinforce the garrison on March 1.

sources.name: William Barret Travis letter, Feb 24, 1836 — Texas State Library
William C. Davis, Three Roads to the Alamo (HarperCollins)

events.title: Fall of the Alamo — All Defenders Killed
events.detail: Before dawn on March 6, 1836, Santa Anna's army assaulted the Alamo in four columns. After roughly 90 minutes of intense fighting, all ~189 Texan defenders were killed, including William Barret Travis, Jim Bowie, and Davy Crockett. Mexican losses were approximately 400–600 killed and wounded. Santa Anna ordered the defenders' bodies burned. The battle became a rallying cry — 'Remember the Alamo!' — that galvanized Texan resistance.

sources.name: Mexican Army official dispatch, Santa Anna to War Ministry, Mar 6, 1836
William C. Davis, Three Roads to the Alamo (HarperCollins)

events.title: Battle of Coleto Creek — Fannin's Force Surrounded
events.detail: James Fannin's 300-man Texan force was intercepted by General José Urrea's Mexican cavalry and infantry on the open prairie near Coleto Creek. Caught in the open without water, the Texans formed a square and repulsed Mexican attacks but suffered heavy casualties. Promised the honors of war, Fannin surrendered on March 20. The prisoners were marched back to Goliad.

sources.name: Harbert Davenport collection — Texas State Library
Stephen L. Hardin, Texian Iliad (University of Texas Press)

events.title: Goliad Massacre — 342 Texan POWs Executed
events.detail: On Palm Sunday, by direct order of Santa Anna invoking a Mexican law mandating execution of pirates, approximately 342 Texan prisoners including wounded soldiers were marched out of Presidio La Bahía and shot. Fannin was among the last executed. A small number escaped. The massacre, committed after surrender, deeply outraged US public opinion and became a second rallying cry alongside the Alamo.

sources.name: Archivo de la Secretaría de Defensa Nacional, Mexico
Stephen L. Hardin, Texian Iliad (University of Texas Press)

events.title: Battle of San Jacinto — Texas Wins Independence
events.detail: In a surprise 18-minute assault on Santa Anna's encamped army near the San Jacinto River, Sam Houston's 910-man Texan force attacked during the Mexican siesta hour. Shouting 'Remember the Alamo! Remember Goliad!', Texans killed approximately 630 Mexican soldiers and captured nearly 700. Houston was wounded. Santa Anna was captured disguised as a common soldier the following day. It was a decisive and total victory.

sources.name: Sam Houston Papers — Texas State Archives
Stephen L. Hardin, Texian Iliad (University of Texas Press)

events.title: Treaties of Velasco — Santa Anna Recognizes Texas
events.detail: As a captive of the Texan government, Santa Anna signed two Treaties of Velasco — one public and one secret. The public treaty ended hostilities and required Mexican withdrawal south of the Rio Grande. The secret treaty promised to work for Texas recognition and a boundary at the Rio Grande in exchange for his freedom. The Mexican government repudiated both treaties, asserting Santa Anna had no authority to sign under duress.

sources.name: Treaties of Velasco text — Texas State Library
Andrés Tijerina, Tejanos and Texas Under the Mexican Flag (Texas A&M University Press)

events.title: Republic of Texas Established — Houston Elected President
events.detail: Texan voters ratified the new constitution and overwhelmingly elected Sam Houston as the first president of the Republic of Texas in September 1836. The same vote showed over 3,500 Texans favoring US annexation versus 91 opposed. Texas maintained de facto independence but Mexico never recognized it. The Republic struggled with debt, recurring Mexican raids, and unresolved relations with Native American tribes.

sources.name: Texas State Library and Archives Commission — Election Records
Randolph B. Campbell, Gone to Texas: A History of the Lone Star State (Oxford University Press)

era: Republic of Texas & Road to War (1836–1845)

events.title: Tejano Allies and Lorenzo de Zavala Join the Revolution
events.detail: Native Texans of Mexican descent — Tejanos — played vital roles in the revolution. Lorenzo de Zavala, a former Mexican governor and constitution-maker, became the Republic's first Vice-President. Juan Seguín led a Tejano cavalry company at San Jacinto. Plácido Benavides and other Tejano leaders sided with the rebels, demonstrating the revolution was not solely an Anglo-American uprising but had significant Mexican roots.

sources.name: Andrés Tijerina, Tejanos and Texas Under the Mexican Flag (Texas A&M University Press)
Texas State Library and Archives Commission

events.title: United States Recognizes the Republic of Texas
events.detail: On his last day in office, President Andrew Jackson extended formal diplomatic recognition to the Republic of Texas. Jackson had deliberately delayed recognition to avoid inflaming sectional tensions and risking war with Mexico before the 1836 election. The recognition was the first step toward eventual annexation but fell short of the immediate union that Texan president Houston and most Texans desired.

sources.name: US Department of State Archives — Diplomatic Recognition Records
Marquis James, The Raven: A Biography of Sam Houston

events.title: Texas Annexation Request Rejected by US
events.detail: President Mirabeau Lamar's Texas government formally requested annexation to the United States in 1837, but President Martin Van Buren's administration declined. Northern congressmen feared adding a large slaveholding state would upset the sectional balance in Congress. Concerns about provoking war with Mexico also contributed. Texas remained an independent republic, accumulating debt and diplomatic uncertainty.

sources.name: US Congressional Record, 25th Congress
David M. Pletcher, The Diplomacy of Annexation (University of Missouri Press)

events.title: Mier Expedition — Texan Raiders Captured in Mexico
events.detail: A Texan expedition of ~700 men crossed the Rio Grande on a punitive raid into northern Mexico and was defeated and captured at Mier by Mexican forces under General Pedro de Ampudia. During an escape attempt, 176 prisoners were recaptured. Santa Anna ordered that every tenth prisoner be killed — Mexican officers placed 17 black beans among 159 white beans; those who drew black beans were shot. The Decimation of Mier further inflamed Texan-Mexican hostility.

sources.name: Texas State Library — Mier Expedition Records
Joseph Milton Nance, Attack and Counterattack (University of Texas Press)

events.title: Tyler's Annexation Treaty Rejected by US Senate
events.detail: President John Tyler negotiated a treaty of annexation with Texas but the US Senate rejected it 35-16 in June 1844, far short of the two-thirds required. Opponents, led by Henry Clay and John Quincy Adams, argued annexation meant war with Mexico and expansion of slavery. The debate over Texas dominated the 1844 presidential election, which James K. Polk won on a platform of Texas annexation and 'Manifest Destiny.'

sources.name: US Senate Journal, 28th Congress, 1st Session
David M. Pletcher, The Diplomacy of Annexation (University of Missouri Press)

events.title: Joint Resolution of Congress Annexes Texas
events.detail: To circumvent the Senate two-thirds requirement, President Tyler and incoming President Polk used a simple majority joint resolution to annex Texas in March 1845. Texas voters and a special convention approved the terms in July 1845. Mexico immediately broke diplomatic relations with the United States, calling annexation an act of aggression. The US-Mexico war was now a near-certainty.

sources.name: Joint Resolution of Congress, March 1, 1845 — US National Archives
David M. Pletcher, The Diplomacy of Annexation (University of Missouri Press)

events.title: Texas Admitted as 28th US State
events.detail: President Polk signed the legislation admitting Texas as the 28th state on December 29, 1845. Texas entered as a slave state, claiming the Rio Grande as its southwestern boundary — a claim Mexico vigorously disputed, insisting the traditional border was the Nueces River approximately 130 miles to the north. This boundary dispute would be the immediate trigger for war.

sources.name: An Act for the Admission of the State of Texas — US Statutes at Large
Randolph B. Campbell, Gone to Texas (Oxford University Press)

events.title: Slidell Mission Fails — Diplomacy Collapses
events.detail: President Polk sent Louisiana politician John Slidell to Mexico City with authority to settle all disputes: recognize the Rio Grande boundary, purchase California and New Mexico for up to $25 million, and assume American claims against Mexico. The Mexican government, under intense political pressure, refused to receive Slidell as a full minister, viewing any negotiation as de facto recognition of Texas annexation. Polk was left without a diplomatic avenue and turned to military pressure.

sources.name: John Slidell correspondence — US Department of State Archives
K. Jack Bauer, The Mexican War 1846–1848 (Macmillan)

events.title: Santa Fe Expedition — Lamar's Disaster
events.detail: Republic of Texas President Mirabeau Lamar dispatched a 320-man commercial and diplomatic expedition to Santa Fe to assert Texas's claimed boundary at the Rio Grande and open trade with New Mexico. The expedition was a catastrophe: lost on the Llano Estacado, harassed by Kiowa raids, and finally captured by Mexican forces. Survivors endured a brutal death march to Mexico City. The expedition deepened Texas-Mexico hostility and embarrassed the Republic.

sources.name: Texas State Library and Archives Commission — Santa Fe Expedition Records
Noel M. Loomis, The Texan-Santa Fe Pioneers (University of Oklahoma Press)

events.title: Woll's Raid — Mexico Briefly Retakes San Antonio
events.detail: General Adrián Woll led a 1,200-man Mexican force across the Rio Grande and captured San Antonio on September 11, 1842, holding it for nine days and capturing several Texas district court officials. Texan militia under Mathew Caldwell defeated Woll at the Battle of Salado Creek on September 18, forcing a Mexican withdrawal. The raid demonstrated that the Republic of Texas remained insecure and Mexico had not abandoned its territorial claims.

sources.name: Texas State Library and Archives Commission — Woll Expedition Records
Joseph Milton Nance, Attack and Counterattack (University of Texas Press)

events.title: General Taylor Advances Army to the Rio Grande
events.detail: On orders from President Polk, General Zachary Taylor marched his Army of Occupation from Corpus Christi south to the north bank of the Rio Grande, directly opposite the Mexican city of Matamoros. Taylor's men began construction of Fort Texas (later Fort Brown) in view of Mexican positions. Mexico viewed this advance into the disputed zone between the Nueces and Rio Grande as an act of invasion. War was now imminent.

sources.name: Zachary Taylor dispatch to Adjutant General, March 1846 — US National Archives
K. Jack Bauer, Zachary Taylor: Soldier, Planter, Statesman (Louisiana State University Press)

events.title: Thornton Affair — First Blood of the War
events.detail: A Mexican cavalry force of approximately 1,600 men crossed the Rio Grande and ambushed a 63-man US dragoon patrol under Captain Seth Thornton north of the river near Rancho de Carricitos. Eleven Americans were killed, five wounded, and 49 captured. Polk used this clash to tell Congress: 'Mexico has passed the boundary of the United States, has invaded our territory and shed American blood upon the American soil.' Critics including Rep. Abraham Lincoln disputed that the site was 'American soil.'

sources.name: President Polk War Message to Congress, May 11, 1846 — US National Archives
K. Jack Bauer, The Mexican War 1846–1848 (Macmillan)

events.title: Battle of Palo Alto — US Artillery Dominates
events.detail: General Arista's 4,000-man Mexican army and Taylor's 2,300 Americans met at Palo Alto prairie north of the Rio Grande. US 'flying artillery' — light, fast-moving cannon — proved devastating against the massed Mexican infantry formations. The Mexicans suffered approximately 250–400 casualties to Taylor's 55. The battle established the pattern of the entire war: superior US artillery repeatedly shattered larger Mexican forces.

sources.name: Zachary Taylor official report, May 1846 — US National Archives
John S. D. Eisenhower, So Far from God: The U.S. War with Mexico (Random House)

events.title: Battle of Resaca de la Palma — Mexicans Routed
events.detail: The day after Palo Alto, Taylor pursued Arista's retreating army into the dense chaparral brush near Resaca de la Palma. In close-quarters fighting the Mexicans were routed; Arista's army broke and fled across the Rio Grande in disarray, abandoning artillery, supplies, and their wounded. US forces captured General La Vega. Total Mexican casualties were approximately 547 killed or wounded; American losses were 122. General Taylor was hailed as 'Old Rough and Ready.'

sources.name: Zachary Taylor official report — US National Archives
John S. D. Eisenhower, So Far from God (Random House)

events.title: US Congress Formally Declares War on Mexico
events.detail: After Polk's war message, the House voted 174-14 and the Senate 40-2 to declare war, but debate was fierce. The bill included a preamble asserting that Mexico had 'shed American blood upon American soil.' Many Whigs, led by Henry Clay and including first-term Congressman Abraham Lincoln, voted for war funding but disputed the pretext, with Lincoln later demanding to know the exact 'spot' where blood was shed. John Quincy Adams and 13 other representatives voted against the war entirely.

sources.name: US Congressional Record, 29th Congress, 1st Session
Amy S. Greenberg, A Wicked War: Polk, Clay, Lincoln, and the 1846 U.S. Invasion of Mexico (Knopf)

events.title: Bear Flag Republic Proclaimed in California
events.detail: American settlers and explorers in the Mexican province of Alta California, encouraged by Captain John C. Frémont and his armed surveyors, seized the town of Sonoma and raised the 'Bear Flag,' declaring an independent 'California Republic.' The revolt was short-lived — within days Commodore Sloat raised the American flag at Monterey. California would eventually be incorporated as a US territory following the war.

sources.name: US National Archives — California Conquest Records
Neal Harlow, California Conquered: The Annexation of a Mexican Province (University of California Press)

events.title: Commodore Sloat Raises US Flag at Monterey, California
events.detail: Commodore John Drake Sloat of the US Pacific Squadron landed sailors and marines at Monterey, Alta California, and raised the American flag, claiming California for the United States. Acting on prior secret instructions and news of war, Sloat's bloodless occupation inaugurated the American conquest of California. Within days US forces occupied San Francisco (Yerba Buena) and other key ports.

sources.name: Commodore Sloat dispatch to Secretary of the Navy, July 1846 — US National Archives
Neal Harlow, California Conquered (University of California Press)

events.title: Kearny's Army of the West Captures Santa Fe
events.detail: Brigadier General Stephen W. Kearny led his 1,700-man 'Army of the West' 900 miles from Fort Leavenworth, Kansas, along the Santa Fe Trail. The Mexican governor Manuel Armijo withdrew without a fight, and Kearny entered Santa Fe without opposition on August 18. He proclaimed US sovereignty over New Mexico and established a territorial government before continuing on to California.

sources.name: Kearny's Proclamation at Santa Fe, August 22, 1846 — US National Archives
Dwight L. Clarke, Stephen Watts Kearny: Soldier of the West (University of Oklahoma Press)

events.title: Battle of Monterrey — Taylor Grants Armistice
events.detail: After a bloody three-day assault on the fortified city of Monterrey, Nuevo León, General Taylor's forces penetrated both flanks while General Worth cut the Saltillo road. Mexican General Pedro de Ampudia surrendered the city, but Taylor controversially granted an 8-week armistice and allowed the Mexican army to withdraw with their weapons. Polk was furious, believing Taylor had let a beaten army escape. The armistice was eventually terminated.

sources.name: Zachary Taylor official report — US National Archives
John S. D. Eisenhower, So Far from God (Random House)

era: Decisive Battles — Taylor's Northern Campaign & California (1846–1847)

events.title: US Navy Occupies Tampico Without Resistance
events.detail: Commodore David Conner's Gulf Squadron occupied the strategically vital port of Tampico after Mexican forces withdrew without fighting. Tampico's harbor would serve as a key staging base for General Scott's Veracruz expedition. Its peaceful capture reflected the deteriorating state of Mexican coastal defenses in the Gulf.

sources.name: Commodore Conner dispatch — US National Archives
K. Jack Bauer, Surfboats and Horse Marines: U.S. Naval Operations in the Mexican War (Naval Institute Press)

events.title: Battle of Brazito — Doniphan's Christmas Victory
events.detail: Colonel Alexander Doniphan's Missouri Volunteers, marching south from Santa Fe toward Chihuahua City, defeated a Mexican force at Brazito (near modern El Paso) on Christmas Day. Without artillery or defensive position, Doniphan's frontiersmen repelled a superior Mexican force in a sharp engagement. The victory opened the road to Chihuahua as part of the broadest geographic campaign in the war.

sources.name: Alexander Doniphan official report — US National Archives
Roger D. Launius, Alexander William Doniphan (University of Nebraska Press)

events.title: Battle of Buena Vista — Taylor's Last Stand
events.detail: Santa Anna marched 20,000 men north from San Luis Potosí to destroy Taylor's 4,800-man army near the hacienda Buena Vista south of Saltillo. After two days of ferocious fighting in which US lines nearly broke, artillery under Captain Braxton Bragg and the Mississippi Rifles under Colonel Jefferson Davis held critical positions. Santa Anna withdrew after suffering ~2,100 casualties to Taylor's ~680. The battle made Taylor a national hero and launched his presidential campaign.

sources.name: Zachary Taylor official report — US National Archives
John S. D. Eisenhower, So Far from God (Random House)

events.title: Battle of Sacramento — Doniphan Takes Chihuahua
events.detail: Doniphan's 900 Missouri Volunteers and a train of 300+ trade wagons defeated a 4,000-man Mexican force entrenched on the Sacramento River north of Chihuahua City. US horse artillery devastated the Mexican position and a cavalry charge completed the rout. Doniphan captured Chihuahua City the following day, having marched nearly 2,000 miles from Fort Leavenworth in one of the most remarkable campaigns in American military history.

sources.name: Alexander Doniphan official report — US National Archives
Roger D. Launius, Alexander William Doniphan (University of Nebraska Press)

events.title: Battle of San Pasqual — Kearny Ambushed in California
events.detail: General Kearny's exhausted 100-man Army of the West was ambushed by 75–160 California lancers under Andrés Pico at San Pasqual, near modern Escondido. In the worst US defeat in California, 18 Americans were killed and 13 wounded — including Kearny himself — by skilled Mexican cavalry lance charges. US forces survived only because of the timely arrival of a naval rescue party from San Diego sent by Commodore Stockton.

sources.name: Kearny dispatch to Adjutant General, December 1846 — US National Archives
Neal Harlow, California Conquered (University of California Press)

events.title: Battles of San Gabriel and La Mesa — California Conquered
events.detail: Commodore Robert Stockton and General Kearny's combined naval-land force defeated Mexican Californio forces at the San Gabriel River (Jan 8) and La Mesa (Jan 9) in the final significant battles of the California campaign. The American column entered Los Angeles on January 10. The Treaty of Cahuenga (Jan 13, 1847) formally ended resistance in California, guaranteeing the Californios' rights.

sources.name: Stockton dispatch — US National Archives
Neal Harlow, California Conquered (University of California Press)

events.title: Wilmot Proviso — Slavery Question Ignites Congress
events.detail: Pennsylvania Congressman David Wilmot attached an amendment to a war appropriations bill prohibiting slavery in any territory acquired from Mexico. The House passed it; the Senate blocked it. The proviso, though never enacted, set off a fierce national debate that prefigured the Civil War, dividing both parties along sectional lines. President Polk privately opposed the proviso as unnecessarily inflammatory but had no power to stop the debate it unleashed.

sources.name: US Congressional Record, 29th Congress, 1st Session
Eric Foner, Free Soil, Free Labor, Free Men (Oxford University Press)

events.title: Veracruz Amphibious Landing — Largest US Seaborne Assault
events.detail: General Winfield Scott's army of 12,000 men landed on the beaches south of Veracruz in specially designed surf boats — the largest amphibious operation in US history to that date. The landing itself was unopposed. Scott's forces encircled the walled city and its castle of San Juan de Ulúa in preparation for a siege. The operation was a masterpiece of joint army-navy coordination, planned meticulously by Scott and Captain Robert E. Lee.

sources.name: Winfield Scott official report — US National Archives
Timothy D. Johnson, Winfield Scott: The Quest for Military Glory (University Press of Kansas)

events.title: Veracruz Bombarded and Surrendered
events.detail: Scott bombarded Veracruz for several days with army siege artillery, naval guns, and shells from naval vessels, firing approximately 6,700 rounds in 88 hours. The city, unable to withstand the bombardment and with no relief force coming, surrendered on March 29. Civilian casualties were significant and prompted protests from foreign consuls. The castle of San Juan de Ulúa surrendered days later. Veracruz became Scott's primary supply base for the march inland.

sources.name: Winfield Scott official report — US National Archives
K. Jack Bauer, The Mexican War 1846–1848 (Macmillan)

events.title: Battle of Cerro Gordo — Scott Flanks Santa Anna
events.detail: Santa Anna positioned his army of ~12,000 men in the mountain pass of Cerro Gordo, blocking the National Highway from Veracruz to Mexico City. Captain Robert E. Lee scouted a secret mule path around the Mexican left flank. US forces led by General David Twiggs attacked across this path, turning the Mexican position. Santa Anna barely escaped on foot as his army collapsed; the US captured 3,000 prisoners and 43 cannon. The road to Mexico City was open.

sources.name: Winfield Scott official report — US National Archives
John S. D. Eisenhower, So Far from God (Random House)

events.title: Scott Occupies Puebla Without Battle
events.detail: Mexico's second-largest city, Puebla, surrendered without resistance when Scott's advance guard arrived. Scott halted here for three months — waiting for reinforcements to replace departing short-term volunteers and for yellow fever (vómito negro) season to pass on the coast. The halt allowed Santa Anna time to reorganize Mexico's defenses around the capital but was strategically necessary. Scott used the pause to train and consolidate his army.

sources.name: Winfield Scott official report — US National Archives
Timothy D. Johnson, Winfield Scott (University Press of Kansas)

events.title: Nicholas Trist Sent to Negotiate Peace
events.detail: President Polk sent State Department chief clerk Nicholas Trist to join Scott's army as peace commissioner, authorized to negotiate on the same terms as the failed Slidell Mission plus acquisition of Baja California and upper California. Trist and Scott initially clashed bitterly but later became close allies in pursuing peace. Trist would play the decisive role in producing the final treaty — even after Polk recalled him.

sources.name: Nicholas Trist Papers — Library of Congress
Robert W. Merry, A Country of Vast Designs: James K. Polk and the Conquest of the American Continent (Simon & Schuster)

events.title: Battles of Contreras and Churubusco — Approach to Mexico City
events.detail: In two battles on successive days, Scott's army shattered three Mexican armies south of Mexico City. At Contreras (Padierna), US forces used a night march through a lava field to attack the Mexican rear, routing General Valencia's force in 17 minutes. At Churubusco, Santa Anna's main force defended the fortified convent but was driven out with massive casualties. The San Patricio Battalion of Irish and immigrant US Army deserters fighting for Mexico made a notable last stand at Churubusco before being overwhelmed and captured.

sources.name: Winfield Scott official report — US National Archives
John S. D. Eisenhower, So Far from God (Random House)

events.title: San Patricio Battalion Prisoners Executed
events.detail: Of 85 San Patricio (Saint Patrick) Battalion members captured at Churubusco, a court-martial condemned 72 to death for desertion. General Scott reduced some sentences; 50 were hanged in two batches — 16 at San Ángel on September 10, and 30 at Mixcoac on September 13. The Mixcoac executions were timed so the condemned had to watch the raising of the American flag over Chapultepec Castle. The San Patricios remain honored as heroes in Mexico to this day.

sources.name: Winfield Scott order book — US National Archives
Robert Ryal Miller, Shamrock and Sword: The Saint Patrick's Battalion in the U.S.-Mexican War (University of Oklahoma Press)

events.title: Armistice Negotiations Fail
events.detail: After Churubusco, Santa Anna proposed a ceasefire, and Scott agreed to an armistice to allow peace talks. Trist and Mexican commissioners met repeatedly but Mexico rejected all territorial cessions. When Scott discovered Mexico was using the armistice to resupply and refortify positions in violation of the terms, he terminated it on September 7. The battles for Mexico City resumed immediately.

sources.name: Nicholas Trist Papers — Library of Congress
K. Jack Bauer, The Mexican War 1846–1848 (Macmillan)

events.title: Battle of Molino del Rey — Costly US Attack
events.detail: Acting on faulty intelligence that Mexico was casting cannon at the Molino del Rey (King's Mill), General Worth's division stormed the fortified complex at dawn. The assault succeeded but at disproportionate cost — 116 Americans killed and 665 wounded, nearly 25% of the attacking force, one of the highest US casualty rates of the war. No cannon foundry was found. Critics viewed it as an avoidable blunder.

sources.name: Winfield Scott official report — US National Archives
John S. D. Eisenhower, So Far from God (Random House)

events.title: Battle of Chapultepec — Los Niños Héroes
events.detail: Chapultepec Castle on its prominent hill was Mexico's military academy and the last major fortification before the capital's gates. After an artillery bombardment on September 12, US forces stormed the castle on September 13 using scaling ladders. Six young military cadets — the 'Niños Héroes' — died defending the castle rather than retreat; one reportedly wrapped himself in the Mexican flag and leaped from the ramparts. The castle fell in a few hours. Santa Anna abandoned Mexico City that night.

sources.name: Winfield Scott official report — US National Archives
Héctor Aguilar Camín and Lorenzo Meyer, In the Shadow of the Mexican Revolution (University of Texas Press)

events.title: Fall of Mexico City — US Flag Over the Halls of Montezuma
events.detail: General Winfield Scott rode into Mexico City's main plaza, the Zócalo, on September 14, 1847 — fulfilling the Marines' Hymn line 'From the Halls of Montezuma.' Santa Anna resigned the presidency and attempted to mount guerrilla operations. A riotous mob attacked US soldiers from rooftops and windows in the city; Scott declared martial law. The occupation of the capital effectively ended organized Mexican military resistance, though guerrilla activity continued for months.

sources.name: Winfield Scott dispatch — US National Archives
Timothy D. Johnson, Winfield Scott (University Press of Kansas)

events.title: California Admitted as 31st US State
events.detail: California was admitted to the Union on September 9, 1850, as a free (non-slave) state — a direct consequence of the Wilmot Proviso debate sparked by the Mexican-American War. Its admission as a free state was part of the Compromise of 1850. The Gold Rush had brought 300,000 people to California by 1855, making it one of the most consequential of America's territorial acquisitions. California's statehood reignited the sectional crisis over slavery expansion that led to the Civil War.

sources.name: California Admission Act — US Statutes at Large, 31st Congress
H. W. Brands, The Age of Gold (Doubleday)

events.title: Trist Defies Recall to Continue Peace Negotiations
events.detail: Frustrated by Mexican political paralysis and fearing more extreme US peace terms if war dragged on, Nicholas Trist refused Polk's order to return to Washington. Trist believed — correctly, as events proved — that only his immediate negotiations could produce an acceptable peace. Scott and Trist's British intermediary, Edward Thornton, helped arrange new talks. Polk was furious but ultimately accepted the treaty Trist negotiated, knowing that Congress and the public wanted peace.

sources.name: Nicholas Trist Papers — Library of Congress
Robert W. Merry, A Country of Vast Designs (Simon & Schuster)

events.title: Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo Signed
events.detail: The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo was signed at the Basilica of Guadalupe north of Mexico City on February 2, 1848. Mexico ceded approximately 525,000 square miles — over half its pre-war territory — including present-day California, Nevada, Utah, most of Arizona and New Mexico, and parts of Colorado, Wyoming, Kansas, and Oklahoma. The US paid Mexico $15 million and assumed $3.25 million in claims by American citizens against Mexico. The Rio Grande became the permanent US-Texas border.

sources.name: Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo — US National Archives and Records Administration
Griswold del Castillo, The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo: A Legacy of Conflict (University of Oklahoma Press)

events.title: Gold Discovered at Sutter's Mill — California Gold Rush Begins
events.detail: Nine days before the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo was signed, James Marshall discovered gold at Sutter's Mill in the Sierra Nevada foothills of California — the future American territory. The discovery, kept quiet for months, triggered the California Gold Rush of 1849, bringing 300,000 migrants to California and accelerating statehood. The gold discovery dramatically validated the acquisition of California and transformed the debate over the war's legacy.

sources.name: Sutter's Mill historical records — California State Library
H. W. Brands, The Age of Gold: The California Gold Rush and the New American Dream (Doubleday)

events.title: US Senate Ratifies Treaty — With Amendments
events.detail: The US Senate ratified the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo 38-14 on March 10, 1848, but stripped several provisions including Article 10, which guaranteed existing land grants under Spanish and Mexican law, and modified Article 9, which protected the citizenship rights of Mexicans remaining in the ceded territory. The Senate's modifications would later deprive many Mexican-heritage landowners of their properties despite the treaty's original promises.

sources.name: US Senate Executive Journal, 30th Congress, 1st Session
Griswold del Castillo, The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (University of Oklahoma Press)

events.title: Mexican Congress Ratifies Treaty at Querétaro
events.detail: Mexico's interim government under Manuel de la Peña y Peña, relocated from US-occupied Mexico City to Querétaro, ratified the amended treaty. The vote was 51-35 in the Chamber of Deputies and 33-4 in the Senate — narrower than the US vote, reflecting deep Mexican anguish over the territorial losses. Extreme nationalists denounced it as treasonous capitulation. Ratifications were formally exchanged on May 30, ending the war.

sources.name: Archivo del Congreso General de la República Mexicana, 1848
Griswold del Castillo, The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (University of Oklahoma Press)

events.title: US Troops Evacuate Mexico City
events.detail: American occupation forces evacuated Mexico City on June 12, 1848, completing their withdrawal to Veracruz for repatriation. Scott had maintained a generally disciplined occupation, but the nearly 9-month presence of a foreign army in the capital left a lasting wound on Mexican national identity. The last US soldiers departed Veracruz by August 1848. Mexico was left to rebuild with a depleted treasury, shattered army, and diminished territory.

sources.name: US Army Adjutant General records — US National Archives
K. Jack Bauer, The Mexican War 1846–1848 (Macmillan)

events.title: Gadsden Purchase — Final Territorial Adjustment
events.detail: US Minister James Gadsden negotiated with Santa Anna's government to purchase an additional 29,670 square miles of northern Sonora and Chihuahua for $10 million, to secure a southern route for a transcontinental railroad. The purchase defined the final US-Mexico border as it exists today. Mexico, still in financial crisis from the war, accepted. The Senate ratified the Gadsden Purchase on June 29, 1854, completing the territorial settlement of the Mexican-American War era.

sources.name: Gadsden Treaty text — US National Archives
Paul Neff Garber, The Gadsden Treaty (University of Pennsylvania Press)
